speaker: interviewer
text: --with survivor Sally Muschel. Today is September 18, 1996. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Wroclaw, we knew that we have to leave. Because it was-- and at this time, I was already, like, going with my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard, Bernard Muschel. And, and we were thinking how-- I really wanted to go very much to go to Israel. Because I felt that this would be my place from now on, since I didn't have anybody. For me, it was very difficult. Every single night, I was daydreaming. And I was crying, putting myself to sleep with the hope-- I was always saying, maybe my mother-- maybe she's sick. Maybe she's gonna-- maybe she have amnesia. Maybe she's going to come back. My father, I knew, will not because they told me. And he was killed the first in the city because he refused to go to Judenrat. He knew the ramification for it. But he really didn't care. He said, I'm not going to work with the Germans. And he was the first one to be killed. So him, I knew he was killed. But my mother, I knew that they took her to Auschwitz. So I always had a-- because she went with my cousins, which cousin-- which one survived. And so I figured maybe, maybe she's someplace. And this was my daydream and night dream. I always put myself to sleep with the hope [SOBBING] I'm sure they're going to find me. I'm sure she's sick. I'm sure something-- I'm sure she's going to find me. And then, when we were in Wroclaw, we were thinking how-- my uncle was thinking how to get out from, from Wroclaw. So now, before-- oh yes, before-- I wanted to go home back to my town. And a cousin of mine, which was-- it was very difficult to travel. You couldn't travel. You couldn't go. But he was-- he was in the army. And he-- somehow, he, he was able to go. So we went to-- for a few days, we traveled to, to my town-- to Dabrowa. I came to Dabrowa. I walked in. During the war, the Gestapo lived in our house. After the war, the-- it's the [NON-ENGLISH], which would have been the president, whichever, like mayor or, or, or mayor-- deputy mayor lived in our house. When I walked in, everything was the same. Only the people [SOBBING] weren't there. Even the painting on the wall was the same. I fainted. And she knew right away who I am because our town was a small town. And they knew my father. And everybody knew that I was sent away. So she knew right away who I am. And then-- and then I-- the business took over-- also a Polack who-- named [NON-ENGLISH]. I went to him to the business. They got all very uneasy. Then I came back. And I remember I went to our super. So he, he, himself, he came, and he says, oh, [NON-ENGLISH] is back, you know. And, and she just closed the door in my eyes. She wouldn't even let me go in. There was there a family. There what about four families in Dabrowa which stayed-- they stayed in Dabrowa. I was with a Jewish family which survived, or they were hidden. And they were there. And I was with them. And then came one Polack, the one which was hiding all those people. And, and they said, you better leave because they're going to kill you here. And 4 o'clock at night, we hired a car. This time were cars already. And I went back to Tarnow. So-- because-- and I was so sorry. Because one of the Polacks told them, the Germans, when they took the apartment, they threw out the library, my, my father's Gemaras and Seforim, they-- the whole library, they threw out. And the Polacks picked up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one Polack came to me, and he says, you know, I could sell you your father's big books. Because this was the Shas, the Gemaras. And I really wanted to get them. And he says, I will sell it to you. So I was thinking, how to carry-- how to take them? I mean, look, you, you can't carry just like this. But then I had-- 4 o'clock in the morning, I had to leave Dabrowa. I couldn't-- and I never went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, my grandchildren went there. And my nephew went there. And-- but I never went back. And this was the time which I came back home, which I found everything untouched, like I would leave yesterday and today come back, but just not the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then later, we went-- since my husband and my brother-in-law were born in Vienna, so they made the transport of German Jews to go over the border with a transport to go to Germany. Because we wanted to go to Israel or to-- so there were-- so we went at night. And we went in this transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to the border, this-- what with the English. Oh, we were traveling-- no, just a second. I'm sorry. Yeah, this time, we were traveling with the wagons and with the trains. And we came to the border. It was English-- on the English side. And the English would not let in. And there were a lot of Polish people which they wanted to come with the Germans. And the German Jews went to the English, and they said, those are not German Jews. Those are Polish Jews. And they would let-- not let us go. They kept us at night. And meantime, at night, the Polish Armia Krajowa-- this is the AK-- was shooting at us. And one man-- which, which it was very hot in the wagons because it was-- and he went out. And they shot him in his leg. The Polish Army was, was shooting at-- because they knew that those are Jews. Anyhow, they would not let us go. The German Jews went. And the English would not let go. One thing which is in my memory. When the English officers said, the world doesn't know what kind-- good thing Hitler did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So again, the anti-Semitism. And then we went back to Wroclaw. And then there was-- we realized we said we were in the kibbutz. And then they came, the-- there was the Bricha. And they started to take a-- but we walked. So you couldn't take anything with you, just a little bit-- attache which you could hold in your hands. And that's how we went illegally through the border through Czechoslovakia. And from Czechoslovakia-- from-- to Vienna, from Vienna to Czechoslovakia; from Czechoslovakia to Germany; [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or perhaps to Czechoslovakia to Vienna, I don't remember-- must have been that because we were-- and then we came to Ainring, to a DP camp. Ainring was on the border of Austria. It was in-- near Berchtesgaden, where Hitler had his palace. And we were in Ainring.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at this time, people were crossing back and forth from Austria. And the first question, what it was, who is alive? And the first question what I was asking, do you maybe know-- do you maybe know that they are someplace? Do you know somebody-- do you know [CRYING] Brindl Michlowitz? Do you know? Do you-- Maybe, [SOBBING] maybe you were with them. But, of course, nobody knew, except my cousin said that they were-- they came with her in Auschwitz. And she, she presumed that they were killed because she never saw them again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went in Ainring there for quite a while. And I, I, I went-- there was a school. And there were children. And I was-- a woman, which was teaching before the war in the [NON-ENGLISH] Academy. And she knew me very well. And she says, Sally, come, you're going to work as my helper. Because I, I love very much children. And I work in Russia in this [NON-ENGLISH] home, like, this orphaned home, like helper.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to work with her. And I really enjoyed the work with the little children, with the Jewish children. And I worked with her for quite a while in Ainring.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in Ainring-- from Ainring, they took us-- they liquidated Ainring. And they took us to Lager Lechfeld. Lager Lechfeld was a German military camp, which were the barracks for the army. And there were small houses, which-- called villa, which the-- what one villa, they divided-- four families. You know, each family had a room. And in Lechfeld, we decided with our husband that we're going to get married. And in Lechfeld, in this camp-- this was not the first wedding in, in DP camp. But in Lechfeld, our wedding was the first wedding. I still remember the wedding. I had some cigarettes. So I went to the-- to the dorf and exchanged the cigarettes for a bridal gown with a bridal veil. We gave some food and cigarettes to a German photographer. So he made us pictures before the wedding, of course. Now the wedding was that we cleared one room-- completely was cleared. And my husband-- you get-- you got-- my husband was working in the kitchen. So he was cooking. We got extra from Joint, extra food. And besides, if you were working, you were getting extra food. And, and I went in the dorf in the bakery. And I was baking. And that's how we got married. And of course, under the chuppah, I insist that they should say 'El male rachamim' [SOBBING] for my parents. They came, [SOBBING] you know, to take the bride down. And I said, I don't care who. [SOBBING] One person is good as the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So neighbors, where they lived with us, were very nice people, they said, would you mind? And we're going to take you. [SOBBING] I said, not at all. To me, doesn't make any difference. So then we got married. And we married in 1948, September 22nd. And, and meantime, the-- my in-laws had-- my mother-in-law had the whole family-- not the whole family, because they perished also, but most of the family were here in the United States. So they sent papers. And also, they sent papers of me as-- not as a wife, but as a daughter. Because as a daughter, they could get right away the paper. Otherwise, wouldn't be able to come. So we were traveling, and-- with a boat. And, and March 15, 1949, we arrived in the United States. And the family met us at Boston. And we came to, to New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came Thursday night. And Monday morning, a uncle of my husband-- I asked if I could get right away a job. An uncle of my husband got me a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to travel. [LAUGHS] So how do you travel? I mean, New York, this city. So I was working on Spring Street downtown. So I knew that I have to go-- that I'm going uptown and downtown.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was memorizing the stations. This was very dramatic for me. I was memorizing the stations. And I figure the name of the station. Like, OK, when I had to go back home, I said that each time I, I, I going to go, I didn't know if I have to go downtown or uptown. So I said, I'm going to go on the train. If it won't be this station, so I'm going to go out. And I'm going to go on the other side to be the other station. But luckily, it was at this station. And this time, we had an apartment in Manhattan on 48th Street and Eighth Avenue-- or Ninth Avenue. Eighth Avenue, I think. And so I had to go uptown. And on-- and I got out on 42nd Street. I think for-- at least for four weeks, I made all kinds of signs to get out on the same entrance, but I never did. But here, I mean, we-- gradually, my husband got a job. We struggled. They had parents still there, but we struggled very much. And the most thing was that I always dream that someday, [SOBBING] somehow, my mother survived. And this was-- for a long time, I used to put myself to sleep with those dreams. Till one day, I was on the subway, [SOBBING] and a woman, which looked in the back exactly like my mother. And she even had the same coat, the navy-blue coat with the black fur. And I ran after her. [SOBBING] And I called her. She turned round, surprised. And of course, I saw that [SOBBING] it's not my mother. [CRYING] This I'm-- [SOBBING] I got sick. I had to wait there on the train. [SOBBING] I couldn't move. I had to sit. People came over [SOBBING] because I got-- and, I guess, different this time, I realized that-- not only realized, I knew no, they are not alive. But till this episode, I always was dreaming maybe they are. Maybe my mother is alive. Maybe my cousin is alive, which I knew she looked like a Aryan. She looked very much as a Polish girl. She was blond. And, and she did-- she was on the-- which I found out later, she was on the Irish papers for quite a long time. And for some reason, she decided that she wants to go in to ghetto for a few days to see the mother and to see everybody. And this time just was the day where there was the Aussiedlung. And she went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was dreaming, maybe she's young, maybe. But this time, after this episode in the train, I-- this time, I realized, [SOBBING] no, I have nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had also another very interesting episode in Ainring. I had a-- I mean, I didn't know my father's family. I didn't know at all. I knew that my father had three sisters. And they all had children. But I never knew their name. And I never-- I never knew them. I mean, I saw them maybe once in my life. So I really didn't know them. When my father went there, this-- what-- they were completely far away from us. And once, my father took me there, which-- on a wedding. I don't remember where. But I remember it was a wedding. And he took me there. And-- because I remember as we were traveling. And so I saw it. But I was so overwhelmed that a little girl-- you know, so I didn't remember. And once, I'm coming and ironing-- I was working, and I'm coming home. I mean, home-- we had a room in the barracks-- and comes-- sits a blond girl. And she sits there and waits. And I'm coming in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she tells me, are you Sally? I said, yes. She says, do you had the family this-and-that-- of this name? So I said, I don't know their name. But I know I-- my father had the sisters. And one of the sisters was-- So she says, do you know that they-- anybody's alive? [SOBBING] I said, I don't know. So she says, I am your cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was already, like, '47 or '48 that they-- she found out. Somebody told her from my town-- and I'm alive, and I'm here. And she started to search for me. And she found out that I am in this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she didn't have money to come. So a person, Matt is his name from my town, gave her the money. And she says, go to her. And so that's how I met her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's now in Israel. She has her family. And we do see each other quite often. I went to every single wedding of her children. We brought her here to our children's wedding. We brought her here to a bar mitzvah of [? Yossi's ?] because this is the only one relative which I have from my father's side. OK, so now we were already in the United States. I just backtracked. And at this time, after this episode, I knew already that this is it. I don't have parents. I am alone. I don't have family. Nobody from my close family is alive. We-- in-- here, I went to school. We were struggling very, very-- it was very, very difficult. I worked in a shop. I-- my husband worked in a shop. And-- but we were so happy then-- we are now free, then we could-- we don't need a lot. We're not in camp anymore. We are-- we don't eat to the kitchen for food, which the UNRRA gave. We were very satisfied. We lived in Crown Heights. And in 1950, the-- my daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brenda. She's named Brindl after my [SOBBING] mother. And was my first child. Two years later, Annie was born, which is Channa, after my maternal grandmother. And the children grew. And slowly, we start to work ourselves up. And my husband did a little bit business, with a little bit-- the children went to school. I was going to school. I was working daytime, going at night to school. I remember I used to go-- I went to Bank Street College. And when I had to go home-- it was, at this time-- now it's near Columbia. But that time, was a terrible neighborhood-- terrible neighborhood. It was downtown-- it was in the village. And when I left that-- place, so I called my husband, and I'm leaving now. And he met me because I was afraid to go from the station. He met me on Kingston Avenue. He left the children for about 15-- half an hour alone, 15 minutes alone, and he picked me up. And I went home. And the children were very proud that I'm going to school. [LAUGHS] They were there if-- whatever mark I got, A or B, they-- there was a family mark because they all helped. They were really-- well, they were-- I explained to them that in order to do something-- I know that I'm taking away you time. Because I had to work, too, because we had to make a living. I'm taking away you time, but someday-- and they were very cooperative. They were very good children. We send them to Hebrew schools. They were very good students. They both-- and, as the time progressed, I finished. I got a very good position in Kingsway Academy, a very good school. I was there in the school about 20 years, and-- till I had the accident, which I fell. And then I couldn't work. I, I, I was about six-- this was a very difficult time for me at this time. Because as I was lying in bed, all my thoughts came. And I went in a very big depression. And this time, I had to have help. I went for help. And I got out of it very well. You know, the children were married already. I had grandchildren. I have now seven grandchildren. And my daughter just celebrated her 25th anniversary. My grandson now is in Israel. My older two granddaughters both were in Israel. Each one was a year. They came back. And now they are in college. One is in Stern College. One is in Queens College. And the other ones are all in school. And thank God, I must say, if I look at my family, that Hitler didn't win. [SOBBING] We won. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back, what helped keep you strong?

speaker: interviewee
text: All time during the war they keep me-- only one thing. I would give up a long time-- just the thought that I would come back home, that I would come back to my parents. Only this thought kept me, because the life of a child alone, it's not life, especially between strangers. Then-- but that kept me alive. That kept me on the-- every single day, it's closer. It's closer. I will come home. And that's probably-- which I suffered so terribly then the disappointment that nobody's [SOBBING] alive. I had so many cousins. I had so many aunts. And we were close with my cousins, and-- because the same town cousins-- nobody. Everybody perished. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a few times difficult times here also. Which I start to think-- and especially when I start to think, I had difficult times. And I had to go for help. And-- but now, the last doctor which I went was-- told me to write. So I write a lot now. And this helps me. This helps me just to write my thoughts, my feelings, how I felt, and how-- and this helped me. And so thank God I'm OK. And I'm strong enough to do-- if I look at my wonderful family, I have a wonderful husband, which I could say adores me. And we are, thank God, financially comfortable. And wonderful children-- I have son in-laws, which are not sons-- which are not son-in-laws. They are more than sons. They are really more than sons. [SOBBING] So thank God for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I know you want to read your poem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think we should switch the tape now. No? OK. I think we should. OK, let's-- we're going to switch tapes because I think your poem will take a few minutes.
